TRANSCRIPT
Okay, so this is how to be a Launch Manager, part two. And this is the most critical
piece right here, so I'm starting off with it. And this is all about client selection.
It's almost like you're going to the horse track and you're betting. When you select
your client, it's about betting on the right horse because at some point they have to
perform if you're going to make it in this business. And really I mentioned there's three
different types of business. Three different ways to do this and one is partnering, two is
coaching and consulting, and three is actually being a launch manager.
If you're just coaching and consulting, then it's probably not as critical that you
pick the right horse, although really it is because coaching someone or consulting with
someone who isn't actually going to follow through and do the work is sort of dismal. But
in the ones where you're actually partnering or you're actually going to manage the launch
and get paid on it - either one of those generally you get paid on a percentage of the
results - you have to pick the right person that you're working with. Otherwise you're just
dead in the water. And this is something I have had incredible success at, and it's one
where I've also had some pretty big failures.
For instance, I had someone who seemed like they were a dream client, they had a
product that looked like it was gonna be a great product, and so I put a lot of time into it.
Everything I talk about when I talk about when I did this, I was working with my brother
Jon. So my brother Jon and myself were working together in this business helping people
with their launches.
And this one we worked and worked and worked and worked, and put in a lot of
work planning out the Prelaunch, planning out the offer, but at the end of the day the
person, he just never could bring the project to fruition. He could never ship it. He could
never get it done. It was all talk. He had this theory he had helped people, he'd done this
work in his own life, but in terms of actually putting it into a finished product and
creating a finished launch, it just kept on taking weeks, and then months, and then
ultimately it never happened. And my brother and I we put a lot of time and effort into it
without making a cent. The product never launched. That was an example of picking the
wrong horse. And that wasn't the only wrong horse we picked. There was at least one
other bad horse that we picked.

But then there's a bunch that we picked that were fantastic. And so here I just want
to walk through some of the things you need to do with client selection.
Now, as I mentioned in the last video, I'm going to be referring to my notes so that
I remember everything because there's a lot here.
So this is, just because a lot of times I see this mostly with people who just get so,
they love the PLF process so much. They watch my launch or they watch someone else's
launch and they fall in love with the process. And they're like, "This is amazing. I want to
do it. I don't have a product but my neighbor, he's like really good at training horses. And
I'm gonna go over and try to get him psyched up about creating a product about training
horses and then we'll launch it together." And that can be good, that could work, but just
because your neighbor knows how to train horses or knows how to meditate or knows
how to teach tennis, doesn't mean they're gonna be a good partner.
Just because she knows someone that's got a big domain of knowledge, that they're
an expert on something, that doesn't necessarily make them a good partner. So I'm gonna
walk through some of the things that do.
One of the things, first of all, you don't want to walk up to someone who's just an
expert and try to explain what this whole entire information marketing world is and get
them from baby steps from the start… get them to create a product, and then ... that's just
too much work. You want to partner with someone who's already got the
product done, or is at least already knows of this industry, and has already put a lot
of work into thinking about what their product would look like. Hopefully
someone who has worked with people, created success for people, hopefully worked with
groups of people and created success for groups of people. Hopefully is at least started
organizing their thoughts. And at least has an outline of what their course or what their
product would look like. Much better if they've actually got the product done. So that's the
bare minimum.
And then you have to make sure it's going to be an offer that sells. So there's a
lot of things out there in the world that might be great concepts or even great products
but no one wants to buy. Because the reality is, there's some things that people are willing
to pay for and some things that people are not willing to pay for. A lot of times creators,
they know exactly what the market needs, but they don't have what the market wants. So
you - at the very first blush, you have to sit down and look at it and say, "Is this something
that has a hungry market?" That's got a market that just wants to buy this product. Will

Launch Partnership Bonus – Client Selection
4
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
this excite the prospect?
The reality is, it doesn’t have to excite the general public, but the intended market,
they have to be excited about it, right? So you have to do some research. If you're not in
that target market, if your neighbor has this great product on training horses, and you
don't know anything about horses, then you're gonna have to dig into that market. You're
gonna have to research it. You're gonna have to go to forums. And you're gonna have to go
to Facebook and you're gonna have to look at competing products and you're gonna judge
where this product can go. Whether it has legs. Whether it will convert. Because you can't
launch a product that doesn't sell.
And the launch process, sometimes, can appear to be pure magic. But it isn't so
magical to try to take a product that's not gonna sell and turn it into a product that sells.
At the end of the day it has to be a good offer, it has to be an offer that people want. And
so you do have to, if you aren't intimately familiar with the market, you will have to do
some research. And that research ahead of time, ahead of doing the deal will be well
worth it. Because you don't want to get three months or six months down the road in
terms of launching a product and find out it just doesn't convert. Now again, if you're just
gonna coach or consult, probably not as important as if you’re partnering or being a PLM,
being a Product Launch Manager. But still research at this stage is important.
Then the next thing is… your partner, whoever you're working with, has to be able
to produce. I mentioned this a little bit earlier but they have to be able to produce
the product. They really have to be able to produce that product. They have to be able to
produce the Prelaunch. So generally these days, Prelaunch is video. It doesn't have to be
video, but most the time it's video. They have to be able to stand in front of the camera
and deliver video. So there are people that… no one's born comfortable in front of a
camera. But it takes some level to be comfortable in front of a camera, but there's some
people that just don't ever get there. That are just so shy, or just some people are so
introverted, or just some people can't express themselves well in speaking.
And then you have to look, well is this person gonna be a good partner? Really
because that's what you're looking at for a partnership. Are they going to be a good
partner here? Or could I find some other talent to put in front of the camera. Now that
sounds good in theory, I can't think of any examples where that's actually happened. It's
pretty rare to take a third person and have that be your camera talent. But it's certainly
possible. You could. But some people ... so you have to find someone who's gonna be able
to produce. You have to find someone who's gonna to be able to sell. Now they don't have
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Launch Partnership Bonus – Client Selection
5
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. 	I 	www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
to be the greatest salesman in the world, they don't have to be able to sell sand in the
desert, they don't have to be some amazing car salesman, enthusiastic, "Yaahrr,"
screaming, they just have to be someone who really believes in their offer who really
believes in their product, then they can stand in front of the camera with conviction - tell
people that, "If you've got a horse, you got to get that horse trained and I can show you
how to train that horse." Or whatever the topic is. You're not looking for slick, but you're
looking for someone who can sell with conviction.
And then at some point depending on how big your launch or your business gets,
you're going to need a team. Now it's possible to launch with no team whatsoever. I
did it in my early days. And then I even had million dollar, multi-million dollar launches
with the most minimal, like one or two people on my team. But at some point you're
gonna need some level of team. Even if it's just customer service and tech. So you have to
look at who's going to be on the team. And it could be the person you're working with,
your client, your partner, or potentially at some point you could actually build up your
own launch team where you're bringing your team to it. But can they bring the team?
If you're going to use partners, who's bringing the partners? Can they bring
the partners? At some point you might build up a relationship with partners but if you're
gonna use partners, if you're gonna use outside partners, will they bring the partners or
will you bring the partners? And then also lists. Do they have a list? Do they have an
email list? That specifically - the email list is way more important than social. If they've
got some huge amazing social following you could possibly turn that into an email list, but
who's going to bring it - who's got the list?
And then this is a really key one. And it's gonna sound crazy when I say it, but this
might be the most important out of all these. Is will they actually pay you? This is
business and sometimes in business you have deals go sideways and people don't get
paid. And I have had times where I've worked with people and not gotten paid what I was
supposed to have gotten paid. So this actually happens. It absolutely happens. I'll talk a
little bit later on about sort of ways you can mitigate that, but you have to really trust this
person. Because if depending on how you do this deal, if the launch really comes off in a
big way (and I've done multi-million dollar launches and one of the launches that I
helped out did more than three million dollars)… I'll get into how to negotiate these deals
and how to put together the deals in a minute… but if you're gonna get paid like 10 or 15
percent on a three-million-dollar launch, that's like 300, 400, near five hundred million
dollars. Are they gonna actually write that check to you? Are they gonna pay you? Can you
trust them to actually give you that money after all the dust settles?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

So, at the end of the day, you're looking to partner with someone who
can produce a product. Who can produce a Prelaunch. Who you want to
work with. Who has got the assets in place to really make it go. And who
you can trust is actually gonna come through and pay you.
Okay, so number one thing, client selection. If you don't get client selection
right, you can't be in this business. There will be a lot of heart break. But just follow these
guidelines to help you pick the right horse. So in the next video, I'm gonna talk about the
types of deals. The way you can put these deals together when it comes to helping other
people with their launches.
